You said: The web project must be hosted on the web.

  1. The web project must be hosted on the web.
  2. The project must satisfy the following requirements:
  4. Content appropriate for your audience, so that they can understand the meaning and directions for this app.
  5. At least one image that relates to the content of the page.
  6. Be laid out and styled according to your plan wireframe(s).
  7. Be responsive and designed with mobile first in mind.
  8. Be visually appealing and easily readable.
  9. Be valid HTML and CSS
  10. Score on Lighthouse at least 90 on Performance and a 96 on Accessibility.

  11. You can also try: https://pagespeed.web.dev/ for performance as well. If you use this site you need to score 95 or above.

  12. The site should have dynamic elements provided by Javascript, your code should show include the following: 1. It should show some level of organization: at a minimum it should have more than one function. 2. It should include DOM interaction: select an element, modify it, and listen for and react to events 3. It should show examples of conditional branching 4. Each of the following should be used: Objects, Arrays and at least one array method usage (forEach, map, reduce, filter, etc)

Jun 29

Claude responded: Got it — this is a fairly standard "build a small responsive site with vanilla JS interactivity" assignment.

Parsed requirements and strategized project ideation approach

Got it — this is a fairly standard "build a small responsive site with vanilla JS interactivity" assignment. Let me break down what's actually being graded, then we can pick a project that makes the JS requirements easy to hit naturally.

HTML/CSS side

  • Hosted live (GitHub Pages is free and easiest for this)
  • Clear, audience-appropriate content + at least one relevant image
  • Matches a wireframe you create first
  • Mobile-first responsive layout
  • Valid HTML/CSS (run through the W3C validator before submitting)
  • Lighthouse: 90+ Performance, 96+ Accessibility (and 95+ on PageSpeed Insights if you use that instead)

JS side — this is really 4 boxes to check:

  1. Code organized into more than one function
  2. DOM interaction: querySelector/getElementById → change something → addEventListener reacting to a real event
  3. At least one if/else or switch (conditional branching)
  4. At least one Object, at least one Array, and at least one array method (forEach, map, filter, reduce, etc.)

The trick is picking a project where those 4 JS requirements happen naturally instead of feeling bolted on — something with a list of items (array of objects) that the user filters, sorts, or interacts with works really well for that.

What kind of thing sounds most appealing to actually build?

Jun 29

Claude responded: No worries — let's just generate options and see what clicks.

Orchestrated brainstorming framework with multiple concept options

No worries — let's just generate options and see what clicks. Here are several that map cleanly onto the JS requirements (array of objects + filtering/sorting + DOM events):

1. Study Flashcard / Quiz App
A set of flashcards (array of objects: {question, answer, category}). User clicks "show answer," clicks next/previous, picks a category filter (.filter()), and you track a score. Conditional branching: right/wrong logic, end-of-quiz message based on score.

2. "What Should I [X]?" Randomizer
E.g. "What should I cook tonight?", "What show should I watch?", "What workout today?" Array of objects ({name, time, difficulty, image}). A button picks one at random or filtered by a dropdown (time available, mood, etc.) using .filter(). Good visual appeal potential with images per item.

3. Personal Budget / Expense Tracker
User adds expenses (object pushed into an array), see a running total (.reduce()), filter by category (.filter()), list renders dynamically (.forEach() or .map()). Conditional branching: flag if over budget, color-code amounts.

4. Packing List / Trip Planner
Pick a trip type (beach, hiking, business) from a dropdown — array of objects per trip type, render the matching packing list. Checkboxes to mark items packed, progress bar updates. Conditional: warn if essentials unchecked.

5. Local "Top Picks" Catalog (restaurants, hiking trails, study spots, games — anything you're into)
Array of objects with name/description/rating/image. Sort by rating, filter by category, click a card to expand details. Very natural fit for "browsable list" + image requirement.

6. Habit / Mood Tracker
Click a button each day to log mood/habit completion (object per entry pushed to array). Show a simple streak count or weekly summary (.reduce() or .filter() for "days completed this week"). Conditional: encouraging message based on streak length.

A few of these (#1, #2, #5) are usually the fastest to build well and look polished for a class project, since the visuals practically design themselves around a list of cards/images.

Do any of these grab you, or should I mix elements from a couple together?

Jun 29

Claude responded: Fun + simple + creative — let's lean into something playful rather than "useful.

Curated playful project ideas and planned interactive selection method

Fun + simple + creative — let's lean into something playful rather than "useful." Here are a few that are genuinely fun to build and use:

1. Mystical Fortune Teller / Magic 8-Ball+
User types a question or just clicks a button, gets a random witty/mystical response from an array of fortunes (objects with {text, mood} so you can theme the styling per mood). Add a shake or glow animation on click. Conditional logic: maybe a "ask again" cooldown, or rare "special" fortune.

2. Pick-a-Path Mini Story
A tiny choose-your-own-adventure — 3-5 short scenes, each with 2 button choices. Each scene is an object ({text, image, choices: [...]}) in an array; clicking a choice swaps the current scene. Very visual, very simple, and conditional branching is baked right into the concept.

3. "Roast or Toast" Random Generator
Click a button, get either a silly compliment or a goofy "roast" (playful, not mean) pulled from arrays. Toggle switch lets user pick which mode. Fun to skin visually — confetti or wobble animation on click.

4. Virtual Pet / Plant Care Clicker
A cartoon pet or plant with a "happiness/water" stat. Buttons to feed/water/play increment or decrement the stat (stored in an object), and the pet's image/mood changes based on conditional thresholds (happy/neutral/sad face). Array could hold a log of actions taken.

5. Mood-to-Playlist (or Mood-to-Snack, Mood-to-Movie) Picker
Click a mood emoji, app filters an array of objects (songs/snacks/movies tagged by mood) using .filter() and shows a random pick from the matches. Image per result.

My personal pick for "fun, simple, creative" with the least risk of scope creep: #1 (Fortune Teller) or #4 (Virtual Pet) — both are tiny in scope but feel alive and satisfying to interact with, and they hit every JS requirement without forcing it.

/* if you are using any Google fonts change the font names below to your fonts. Any spaces in your font name should be replaced with a +. Fonts are separated by a | */ @import url("https://fonts.googleapis.com/css?family=IM+Fell+French+Canon+SC|Lato");

:root { /* change the values below to your colors from your palette */ --primary-color: #396e94; --secondary-color: #e7c24f; --accent1-color: #a42212; --accent2-color: white;

/* change the values below to your chosen font(s) */ --heading-font: "IM Fell French Canon SC"; --paragraph-font: Lato, Helvetica, sans-serif;

/* these colors below should be chosen from among your palette colors above */ --headline-color-on-white: var( --primary-color ); /* headlines on a white background */ --headline-color-on-color: white; /* headlines on a colored background */ --paragraph-color-on-white: var( --primary-color ); /* paragraph text on a white background */ --paragraph-color-on-color: white; /* paragraph text on a colored background */ --paragraph-background-color: var(--primary-color); --nav-link-color: var(--primary-color); --nav-background-color: var(--secondary-color); --nav-hover-link-color: white; --nav-hover-background-color: var(--primary-color); }

/* Look around below...but DON'T CHANGE ANYTHING! */

body { max-width: 1200px; margin: 0 auto; padding: 4em; font-size: 18px; } img { display: block; margin: 0 auto; } h1, h2, h3, h4, h5, h6 { font-family: var(--heading-font); color: var(--headline-color-on-white); } h2 { text-align: center; } hr { height: 3px; margin: 35px 0; background: var(--accent1-color); } header { padding: 1em; text-align: center; color: var(--paragraph-color-on-color); background-color: var(--paragraph-background-color); } header > h1, header > h2 { color: var(--headline-color-on-color); }

p { font-family: var(--paragraph-font); color: var(--paragraph-color); padding: 1em; } .colors { width: 100%; min-width: 350px; margin: 30px auto; text-align: center; } .colors th { background-color: #999; } .colors td { width: 25%; height: 3em; }

.primary { background-color: var(--primary-color); } .secondary { background-color: var(--secondary-color); } .accent1 { background-color: var(--accent1-color); } .accent2 { background-color: var(--accent2-color); }

p.colored { background-color: var(--paragraph-background-color); color: var(--paragraph-color-on-color); }

nav { background-color: var(--nav-background-color); line-height: 3em; text-align: center; font-size: 1.2em; } nav { list-style-type: none; display: flex; } nav a { padding: 1em; min-width: 120px; text-decoration: none; padding: 10px; } nav a:link, nav a:visited { color: var(--nav-link-color); } nav a:hover { color: var(--nav-hover-link-color); background-color: var(--nav-hover-background-color); }

.sitemap { display: grid; justify-content: center;

grid-template-columns: repeat(6, 15%); grid-template-rows: 3em 1.5em 1.5em 3em; grid-template-areas: ". . home home . ." ". . . top . ." ". left left right right ." "page2 page2 . . page3 page3"; } .sitemap > div { text-align: center; } .sm-home { grid-area: home; background-color: #ccc; line-height: 3em; } .sm-page2 { grid-area: page2; background-color: #ccc; line-height: 3em; } .sm-page3 { grid-area: page3; background-color: #ccc; line-height: 3em; }

.top { grid-area: top; border-left: 1px solid; }

.left { grid-area: left; border-top: 1px solid; border-left: 1px solid; }

.right { grid-area: right; border-top: 1px solid; border-right: 1px solid; }

actually let me know what i need to do for this assignment to submit it:

Final Project - Planning ▶ Requirements for the project you will be planning Project Requirements The web project must be hosted on the web. The project must satisfy the following requirements: Content appropriate for your audience, so that they can understand the meaning and directions for this app. At least one image that relates to the content of the page. Be laid out and styled according to your plan wireframe(s). Be responsive and designed with mobile first in mind. Be visually appealing and easily readable. Be valid HTML and CSS Score on Lighthouse at least 90 on Performance and a 96 on Accessibility. You can also try: https://pagespeed.web.dev/ for performance as well. If you use this site you need to score 95 or above. The site should have dynamic elements provided by Javascript, your code should show include the following: It should show some level of organization: at a minimum it should have more than one function. It should include DOM interaction: select an element, modify it, and listen for and react to events It should show examples of conditional branching Each of the following should be used: Objects, Arrays and at least one array method usage (forEach, map, reduce, filter, etc) Activity Instructions In the previous weeks you should have come up with an idea for your personal web application. Before we build our personal (final) project we need to do some planning. You have seen site plans used to build other projects. They contain many of the details we needed to know in order to build sites. We should prepare a similar document for our personal application now. File Management: Let's start by adding a folder within the 'wdd131' folder. You can do this in file management or on Visual Studio Code. Give this folder a name and remember this will be a part of the URL (web address), so keep it short, simple, and professional. The folder name should be all lowercase with no spaces. The name should also have something to do with the topic of your project. This folder will be at the root level of your directory and will contain the new project. Add a file to your new folder called app-plan.html. Instructions: You have two options for your app plan. One option is to create your own app plan from scratch. You are not required to use a template. For either option you need to provide the following for your app-plan: purpose, audience, dynamic elements used, pseudocode (planned out logic of the code), and wireframes. The other option is to use an existing template by going to Web App Plan Template, right-click on the page, and view the source code. (On Chrome it's the pop-up menu option called 'View Page Source'.) To use the template, copy all the HTML code. You can use Cmd+A or Ctrl+A to select it all quickly, then copy it. Paste the code into your site-plan.html page in VSCode and save the file. Create a new 'styles' folder. And create a site-plan.css file inside that folder. Go to: Site Plan CSS and copy all the code. Paste the code into the site-plan.css file in VSCode and save it. Then complete each section. Purpose and Audience In 2–3 sentences, write a purpose statement for your site and write about your intended audience. Description of the Dynamic Elements Describe how you will use Javascript to add dynamic elements to your site. Pseudocode - Computational Thinking Write the out the logically steps of how you plan to code your application. Wireframe Create a wireframe for your project. Add your wireframe as an image or link to the site plan. If there are multiple pages, include one wireframe for each page. Submit As soon as you have this plan completed submit it! The sooner you get it approved the sooner you can get started building your project. Don't wait until the last minute on this one! Remember, we don't have an index.html or home page inside our personal site project folder yet. So you have to type in the app-plan.html at the end of the URL web address and select Enter to get it to open. Something like this:

your-account.github.io/wdd131/personal-folder/app-plan.html

Your web app plan should then come up. Use that URL to submit. Submit the GitHub link to your site-plan.html page to I-Learn.

Claude responded: Good catch wanting to slow down — this assignment is just the planning phase, not the actual build.

Synthesized assignment steps and drafted budget tracker planning content

Good catch wanting to slow down — this assignment is just the planning phase, not the actual build. Here's exactly what's expected, followed by the written content you need for it.

What to actually do, step by step

  1. Make a project folder inside your wdd131 folder. Lowercase, no spaces, short, related to the topic — something like budget or budgettracker.
  2. Inside that folder, create app-plan.html — that's the filename the instructions name in step one.
  3. Get your plan structure, either by writing your own HTML from scratch, or by opening the "Web App Plan Template" page, right-clicking → "View Page Source," and copying all of it into app-plan.html.
  4. Create a styles folder inside your project folder, and inside that create a CSS file for the plan page, then paste in the provided "Site Plan CSS" code. ⚠️ One thing to flag: your instructions name the file app-plan.html at the start, but later say to paste the template into site-plan.html and create site-plan.css. That's almost certainly leftover wording reused from a different week's "site plan" assignment. I'd stick with app-plan.html (matching the URL example at the bottom of your instructions) for the page, and you can still call the stylesheet site-plan.css like the instructions say — just make sure app-plan.html links to it correctly. If you're unsure, a quick message to your instructor confirming the filename takes 30 seconds and saves a headache.
  5. Fill in the four required sections in app-plan.html:
    • Purpose and Audience
    • Description of the Dynamic Elements
    • Pseudocode
    • Wireframe (image or link)
  6. Push it to GitHub (commit + push to the repo that powers your-account.github.io).
  7. Test the live URL directly — since there's no index.html in that folder yet, you have to type the full path:
    your-account.github.io/wdd131/your-folder-name/app-plan.html
  8. Submit that URL to I-Learn.

Report: Personal Budget Tracker

Here's writeup content for the Purpose and Audience and Description of Dynamic Elements sections — adapt the name/wording as you like, then paste into your plan.

Purpose & Audience

This app is a simple, browser-based budget tracker that lets a user set a spending limit, log individual expenses against it, and immediately see how much money they have left. The purpose is to replace mental math or scattered notes with one clear, visual snapshot of spending. The intended audience is students and young adults managing a limited weekly or monthly amount — like spending money, groceries, or entertainment funds — who want something fast and login-free to check from their phone.

Description of Dynamic Elements

JavaScript drives all of the page's interactivity. Expenses are stored as objects (description, amount, category) inside an array; submitting the "add expense" form triggers an event listener that creates a new expense object, pushes it into the array, and re-renders the list. The running total is calculated with the array's reduce() method, and the remaining balance updates and changes color (conditional branching) as the user nears or exceeds their budget. A category filter uses filter() to show only matching expenses, and a delete button on each entry removes it from the array and the DOM. A visual progress bar ("budget jar") fills based on percentage of budget spent, giving an at-a-glance sense of where they stand.

What the page will contain

  • Header with app name/tagline
  • Budget-setup control (set total budget amount)
  • Visual progress indicator ("jar" or bar) showing spent vs. remaining
  • Add-expense form (description, amount, category)
  • Dynamically rendered expense list/ledger, each entry removable
  • Category filter buttons
  • Summary stats: total spent, remaining balance, % of budget used
  • At least one relevant image (e.g., a jar, wallet, or piggy bank graphic)
  • Footer with name/last-updated date

Want me to also draft the pseudocode section next, or help sketch the wireframe?

Jun 29

Claude responded: Here's pseudocode covering the full flow — structured so each section maps to one of the JS requirements (organization/functions, DOM interaction, conditionals…

Determined inline pseudocode format for budget tracker app

Here's pseudocode covering the full flow — structured so each section maps to one of the JS requirements (organization/functions, DOM interaction, conditionals, objects/arrays/array methods).

INITIALIZE
  SET budgetTotal = 0
  SET expenses = []  // array of expense objects: {id, description, amount, category}
  SELECT form, budgetInput, expenseList, totalDisplay, remainingDisplay, progressBar, filterButtons

ON PAGE LOAD
  DISPLAY empty state message ("No expenses yet")
  SET progress bar to 0%

FUNCTION setBudget(amount)
  IF amount is empty OR amount <= 0 THEN
    SHOW error message
  ELSE
    SET budgetTotal = amount
    CALL updateSummary()

FUNCTION addExpense(description, amount, category)
  IF description is empty OR amount <= 0 THEN
    SHOW validation error
    RETURN
  CREATE newExpense = { id: uniqueId, description, amount, category }
  PUSH newExpense INTO expenses array
  CALL renderExpenseList()
  CALL updateSummary()
  CLEAR form inputs

FUNCTION renderExpenseList(filteredCategory = "all")
  CLEAR expenseList element
  IF filteredCategory == "all" THEN
    SET itemsToShow = expenses
  ELSE
    SET itemsToShow = expenses.filter(expense => expense.category == filteredCategory)
  
  IF itemsToShow.length == 0 THEN
    DISPLAY "No expenses in this category"
  ELSE
    FOR EACH expense IN itemsToShow (using forEach)
      CREATE list item with description, amount, category, delete button
      APPEND list item to expenseList

FUNCTION calculateTotalSpent()
  RETURN expenses.reduce((sum, expense) => sum + expense.amount, 0)

FUNCTION updateSummary()
  SET totalSpent = calculateTotalSpent()
  SET remaining = budgetTotal - totalSpent
  SET percentUsed = (totalSpent / budgetTotal) * 100

  UPDATE totalDisplay text = totalSpent
  UPDATE remainingDisplay text = remaining

  // conditional branching on budget status
  IF remaining < 0 THEN
    SET status = "over budget"
    SET progressBar color = red
  ELSE IF percentUsed >= 80 THEN
    SET status = "approaching limit"
    SET progressBar color = yellow
  ELSE
    SET status = "on track"
    SET progressBar color = green

  UPDATE progressBar width = percentUsed + "%"
  DISPLAY status message based on status

FUNCTION deleteExpense(id)
  SET expenses = expenses.filter(expense => expense.id != id)
  CALL renderExpenseList()
  CALL updateSummary()

EVENT LISTENERS
  ON form submit:
    PREVENT default page reload
    READ values from description/amount/category inputs
    CALL addExpense(description, amount, category)

  ON budgetInput change:
    CALL setBudget(budgetInput.value)

  ON click of any delete button (event delegation):
    GET expense id from clicked element
    CALL deleteExpense(id)

  ON click of filter button:
    GET selected category from button
    CALL renderExpenseList(category)

This hits every requirement cleanly: multiple distinct functions, real DOM select/modify/listen-react cycles, if/else branching for budget status and validation, and an expenses array of objects used with .reduce(), .filter(), and .forEach().

A few things still need your input before this is submit-ready:

  1. Your name and section — fill in the two placeholders near the top
  2. Logo image — even a simple jar icon works; add the file path to the empty src=""

To get this live:

  • Drop app-plan.html into your new project folder (e.g. wdd131/budgetjar/)
  • Put site-plan.css inside a styles subfolder there
  • Commit and push to GitHub
  • Visit your-account.github.io/wdd131/budgetjar/app-plan.html to confirm it renders, then submit that URL

add this as well:

Purpose & Audience This app is a simple, browser-based budget tracker that lets a user set a spending limit, log individual expenses against it, and immediately see how much money they have left. The purpose is to replace mental math or scattered notes with one clear, visual snapshot of spending. The intended audience is students and young adults managing a limited weekly or monthly amount — like spending money, groceries, or entertainment funds — who want something fast and login-free to check from their phone. Description of Dynamic Elements JavaScript drives all of the page's interactivity. Expenses are stored as objects (description, amount, category) inside an array; submitting the "add expense" form triggers an event listener that creates a new expense object, pushes it into the array, and re-renders the list. The running total is calculated with the array's reduce() method, and the remaining balance updates and changes color (conditional branching) as the user nears or exceeds their budget. A category filter uses filter() to show only matching expenses, and a delete button on each entry removes it from the array and the DOM. A visual progress bar ("budget jar") fills based on percentage of budget spent, giving an at-a-glance sense of where they stand. What the page will contain Header with app name/tagline Budget-setup control (set total budget amount) Visual progress indicator ("jar" or bar) showing spent vs. remaining Add-expense form (description, amount, category) Dynamically rendered expense list/ledger, each entry removable Category filter buttons Summary stats: total spent, remaining balance, % of budget used At least one relevant image (e.g., a jar, wallet, or piggy bank graphic) Footer with name/last-updated date INITIALIZE SET budgetTotal = 0 SET expenses = [] // array of expense objects: {id, description, amount, category} SELECT form, budgetInput, expenseList, totalDisplay, remainingDisplay, progressBar, filterButtons ON PAGE LOAD DISPLAY empty state message ("No expenses yet") SET progress bar to 0% FUNCTION setBudget(amount) IF amount is empty OR amount <= 0 THEN SHOW error message ELSE SET budgetTotal = amount CALL updateSummary() FUNCTION addExpense(description, amount, category) IF description is empty OR amount <= 0 THEN SHOW validation error RETURN CREATE newExpense = { id: uniqueId, description, amount, category } PUSH newExpense INTO expenses array CALL renderExpenseList() CALL updateSummary() CLEAR form inputs FUNCTION renderExpenseList(filteredCategory = "all") CLEAR expenseList element IF filteredCategory == "all" THEN SET itemsToShow = expenses ELSE SET itemsToShow = expenses.filter(expense => expense.category == filteredCategory)

IF itemsToShow.length == 0 THEN DISPLAY "No expenses in this category" ELSE FOR EACH expense IN itemsToShow (using forEach) CREATE list item with description, amount, category, delete button APPEND list item to expenseList FUNCTION calculateTotalSpent() RETURN expenses.reduce((sum, expense) => sum + expense.amount, 0) FUNCTION updateSummary() SET totalSpent = calculateTotalSpent() SET remaining = budgetTotal - totalSpent SET percentUsed = (totalSpent / budgetTotal) * 100 UPDATE totalDisplay text = totalSpent UPDATE remainingDisplay text = remaining // conditional branching on budget status IF remaining < 0 THEN SET status = "over budget" SET progressBar color = red ELSE IF percentUsed >= 80 THEN SET status = "approaching limit" SET progressBar color = yellow ELSE SET status = "on track" SET progressBar color = green UPDATE progressBar width = percentUsed + "%" DISPLAY status message based on status FUNCTION deleteExpense(id) SET expenses = expenses.filter(expense => expense.id != id) CALL renderExpenseList() CALL updateSummary() EVENT LISTENERS ON form submit: PREVENT default page reload READ values from description/amount/category inputs CALL addExpense(description, amount, category) ON budgetInput change: CALL setBudget(budgetInput.value) ON click of any delete button (event delegation): GET expense id from clicked element CALL deleteExpense(id) ON click of filter button: GET selected category from button CALL renderExpenseList(category)

Claude responded: Great questions — let me fix the wireframe first, then answer the time question.

Assessed wireframe spacing issue and validated project feasibility within timeframe

Great questions — let me fix the wireframe first, then answer the time question.

On time: yes, totally doable in 3–4 hours. The wireframe looks detailed because it's a wireframe showing every element, but the actual app is just one HTML file, one CSS file, one JS file. The core features (set budget, add expense, show list, delete, update total) can be built in 2 hours. The filter buttons and progress bar are maybe another hour. The styling polishes it in the last hour. You don't need notes fields, multiple categories, or a fancy jar animation — keep it simple and it's very manageable.

Now fixing the mobile — pulling the footer right up after the last row:

V

visualize

V

visualize show_widget

Mobile footer is now right under Netflix with no gap.

On the time question — here's a realistic breakdown for 3–4 hours:

  • ~30 min — HTML structure (header, form, list area, footer)
  • ~30 min — CSS styling using your palette (mobile-first, then one media query for desktop)
  • ~90 min — JavaScript (add expense, delete, calculate total, update display)
  • ~30 min — filter buttons + progress bar fill
  • ~30 min — final polish, Lighthouse check, deploy to GitHub Pages

That's right at 3.5 hours if you follow the pseudocode we already wrote. The idea is perfectly scoped — don't change it.

Want me to generate the actual index.html with the starter structure so you can jump straight to coding?
